title: Performance
description: Recomposition optimization, memory leaks, profiling, Macrobenchmark, Compose compiler metrics, and APK size
lesson:
Compose Performance

Compose is fast by default, but you can make it faster — or accidentally make it slow.

Recomposition Rules

  • Compose skips recomposition when all parameters are stable and unchanged
  • Stable types: primitives, String, data classes, @Immutable/@Stable annotated classes
  • Unstable: regular classes, List (not SnapshotStateList), lambdas capturing mutable values

Common Pitfalls

  • Singleton objects holding Activity references → memory leaks
  • Inline lambdas creating new instances every recomposition
  • Reading state at too broad a scope

Tools

Layout Inspector shows recomposition counts. Baseline Profiles pre-compile hot paths for faster cold starts. LeakCanary detects memory leaks automatically.

Macrobenchmark

Measures real-world performance on a real device:

@RunWith(AndroidJUnit4::class) class StartupBenchmark { @get:Rule val benchmarkRule = MacrobenchmarkRule() @Test fun startupCompilationFull() = benchmarkRule.measureRepeated( packageName = "com.example.app", metrics = listOf(StartupTimingMetric()), iterations = 5, startupMode = StartupMode.COLD ) { pressHome() startActivityAndWait() } }
Metrics available: StartupTimingMetric (TTID/TTFD), FrameTimingMetric (jank), TraceSectionMetric (custom trace points), PowerMetric (battery). Macrobenchmark also auto-generates Baseline Profiles.

Compose Compiler Reports

The Compose compiler can report which classes are stable/unstable and which composables skip recomposition:

// Enable in build.gradle.kts kotlinOptions { freeCompilerArgs += listOf( "-P", "plugin:androidx.compose.compiler.plugins.kotlin:reportsDestination=" + project.buildDir.absolutePath + "/compose_metrics" ) } // Output: app_release-composables.txt restartable skippable scheme("[...]") fun UserCard( stable user: User stable onSelect: Function1<String, Unit> ) // "skippable" = good! Compose can skip recomposition // If you see "restartable" without "skippable" = problem

APK Size Reduction

  • R8 shrinking — Removes unused code: minifyEnabled = true
  • Resource shrinking — Removes unused resources: shrinkResources = true
  • WebP — Convert PNGs to WebP (25-35% smaller)
  • App Bundle — Upload AAB, Google Play generates optimized APKs per device (saves ~15%)
  • Dynamic feature modules — Download features on demand

questions.title: Causes of Recomposition
questions.description: Which causes unnecessary recompositions in Compose?
options: Reading a State value inside a composable
Passing an unstable type (class without equals/hashCode) as a parameter
Using remember{} to cache computations
Using LazyColumn with a key parameter
questions.explanation: Compose skips recomposition when parameters are stable and unchanged. Unstable types (without proper equals/hashCode) force recomposition every time because Compose can't tell if they changed. Use data class or annotate with @Stable/@Immutable.
hints: How does Compose decide whether to skip recomposition?

questions.title: Unstable Lambda Capture
questions.description: This recomposes ItemRow every time the parent recomposes. Optimize it.
questions.explanation: Inline lambdas create new instances every recomposition, making them "unstable" parameters. remember(item.id) { { ... } } caches the lambda and only recreates it when item.id changes.
hints: Every recomposition creates new lambda objects

questions.title: Memory Leak
questions.description: This code leaks memory. The Activity is never garbage collected after rotation. Fix it.

checks.message: Clean up the listener in onDestroy

questions.explanation: The anonymous inner class holds a reference to MainActivity. Since the singleton lives forever, the old Activity can't be garbage collected after rotation. Fix: clear the listener in onDestroy().
hints: Singleton objects live for the entire app process

questions.title: Baseline Profiles
questions.description: What are Baseline Profiles and how do they improve performance?
questions.answer: Baseline Profiles are lists of critical classes and methods that should be AOT-compiled during installation. They improve cold startup time (up to 30%) by ensuring hot code paths are native code when the app launches.
questions.explanation: Without Baseline Profiles, ART uses interpretation and JIT compilation. Cold starts are slow. Baseline Profiles tell the system which methods to AOT-compile at install time. The Macrobenchmark library generates them automatically.
hints: What if the system knew which methods were important before the first launch?

questions.title: Bitmap Memory Leak
questions.description: This image loading code causes OutOfMemoryError with large images. Fix it to load images efficiently.

checks.message: Downsample the bitmap or use an image loading library

questions.explanation: A 12MP photo is ~48MB in memory as a Bitmap. inJustDecodeBounds = true reads dimensions without loading pixels. inSampleSize downsamples (2 = half size = 1/4 memory). In practice, use Coil or Glide which handle this automatically with caching, lifecycle awareness, and Compose integration.
hints: You can read dimensions without loading the full bitmap

questions.title: R8 vs ProGuard
questions.description: What is the relationship between R8 and ProGuard in modern Android development?
options: They are the same tool with different names
R8 replaced ProGuard as the default — it does the same things (shrink, optimize, obfuscate) but faster, and uses ProGuard rule syntax
ProGuard handles code, R8 handles resources
R8 is only for Kotlin, ProGuard is for Java
questions.explanation: R8 is Google's replacement for ProGuard. It performs the same functions (code shrinking, optimization, obfuscation) but is faster and produces smaller output. It uses the same rule syntax as ProGuard (proguard-rules.pro), making migration seamless. R8 has been the default since AGP 3.4.
hints: R8 is backward-compatible with ProGuard rules

questions.title: Macrobenchmark Startup Modes
questions.description: What is the difference between COLD, WARM, and HOT startup in Macrobenchmark?
options: COLD = first install, WARM = update, HOT = cached
COLD = process killed, app started fresh; WARM = process alive, activity destroyed; HOT = activity paused and resumed
COLD = no network, WARM = Wi-Fi, HOT = 5G
They all measure the same thing with different iteration counts
questions.explanation: COLD: Process is dead — full startup from scratch (Zygote fork → Application → Activity). Most expensive, most realistic for first launch. WARM: Process is alive but Activity was destroyed — no Application.onCreate(). HOT: Activity exists, just brought back to foreground — cheapest. COLD start is the most important to optimize (Baseline Profiles, reducing ContentProvider inits).
hints: Think about what Android recreates at each level

questions.title: Compose Compiler Report
questions.description: What does this Compose compiler report tell you about the composable?
questions.code:
restartable scheme("[...]") fun ItemCard(
  unstable items: List<Item>
  stable title: String
  stable onSelect: Function1<String, Unit>
)
options: ItemCard is fully optimized and will skip recomposition when inputs haven't changed
ItemCard is restartable but NOT skippable because 'items' is unstable (List is not stable by default) — it recomposes every time the parent recomposes even if data hasn't changed
ItemCard will crash at runtime due to the unstable parameter
The report shows that ItemCard uses too much memory
questions.explanation: The report shows restartable but NOT skippable — this is a performance problem. The culprit is unstable items: List<Item>. Kotlin's List is an interface that could be a MutableList at runtime, so Compose can't guarantee it hasn't changed. Fix options: (1) Use @Immutable or @Stable on Item, (2) Use kotlinx.collections.immutable.ImmutableList, (3) Use SnapshotStateList.
hints: A composable needs ALL stable parameters to be 'skippable'
List is unstable because it's an interface — it could be mutable

questions.title: APK Size Reduction
questions.description: Which combination of settings enables both code and resource shrinking for a release build?
options: proguardFiles only
minifyEnabled = true and shrinkResources = true
shrinkResources = true alone (it implies minifyEnabled)
debuggable = false enables all optimizations automatically
questions.explanation: Both must be set explicitly. minifyEnabled = true enables R8 code shrinking (removes unused classes/methods). shrinkResources = true removes unused resources (images, layouts). Important: shrinkResources requires minifyEnabled — it can't work alone because it needs R8 to determine which resources are referenced in code. Together they can reduce APK size by 20-50%.
hints: shrinkResources depends on minifyEnabled — order matters

questions.title: Doze Mode & Battery
questions.description: What happens to your app's background work when the device enters Doze mode?
options: All background work is permanently killed
Network access, jobs, syncs, and alarms are deferred to periodic maintenance windows
Only GPS is disabled, everything else continues
Doze mode only affects apps targeting API 23+
questions.explanation: Doze mode restricts: network access, JobScheduler/WorkManager jobs, syncs, standard alarms, and wake locks. Work is deferred to periodic maintenance windows where the system briefly exits Doze. setExactAndAllowWhileIdle() alarms still fire but are rate-limited. Firebase Cloud Messaging high-priority messages can wake the device. Doze deepens over time — longer idle = longer between maintenance windows.
hints: Doze doesn't kill work — it defers it to save battery

questions.title: Non-Skippable Composable
questions.description: The Compose compiler report shows this composable is NOT skippable. Fix it so Compose can skip recomposition when data hasn't changed.

checks.message: Make the data class stable for Compose

questions.explanation: Two changes make this skippable: (1) @Immutable on UiState tells Compose the class won't change after construction. (2) ImmutableList (from kotlinx-collections-immutable) replaces List — Compose knows it's truly immutable. Regular List is unstable because the interface could be backed by a MutableList. With both changes, the Compose compiler marks ItemList as skippable.
hints: The issue is List<Item> — Compose can't trust it won't mutate

questions.title: 16ms Frame Budget
questions.description: Explain the 16ms frame budget. What happens when a frame takes 20ms? How would you use systrace/Perfetto to identify whether the bottleneck is in measure, layout, draw, or the render thread?
questions.answer: At 60fps, each frame has 16.67ms to complete. If a frame takes 20ms, it misses the VSYNC deadline — the previous frame is displayed for an extra cycle (33ms), causing visible jank. In Perfetto/systrace, look at the 'Choreographer#doFrame' trace section: it shows INPUT, ANIMATION, and TRAVERSAL phases. Within TRAVERSAL, you'll see measure, layout, and draw timing. If 'draw' is fine but the frame is still late, check the RenderThread track — GPU work may be the bottleneck. Common causes: nested layout weight (double measure), heavy onDraw(), or overdraw.
questions.explanation: The VSYNC signal drives frame rendering. At 60fps each frame has ~16ms. The Choreographer schedules work in phases: input handling, animations, then view traversals (measure/layout/draw). Perfetto shows each phase's duration, making it easy to pinpoint the bottleneck. The RenderThread track reveals GPU-side issues. Look for frames where the Choreographer#doFrame slice exceeds 16ms.
hints: Perfetto has separate tracks for the UI thread and RenderThread

learnMore.title: Android Developers — Inspect Performance with Perfetto
